title: Freehand: a page that makes sense without eyes
why.empty: Empty editor. Do not start from the code. Draw the page on paper — rectangles. Then name each one. The code is just writing the drawing down.
why.no-header: There is no top part. The logo and the site title sit somewhere — but the browser does not know that is the top part unless you tell it.
why.no-nav: There is no navigation. A list of links is not a menu until you call it a menu.
why.no-main: There is no main content. If everything on the page is equally important, then nothing is. Say which part is the point.
why.no-article: There are no standalone things. The task asks for at least two — articles, listings, recipes. Something that would still make sense on its own, lifted out of the page.
why.no-footer: There is no bottom part. It is not decoration — it is where the content ends and the formalities begin.
why.not-closed: A tag is open and never closed, or closed in the wrong order.
blocks.2.text: Your topic, again. But this time you have one extra condition, and it is the whole test:
blocks.3.text: Write the page so that someone could understand it without seeing it.
blocks.4.text: It must have: a top part with a logo or site title, navigation, main content, at least two standalone things inside it (articles, listings, recipes — whatever fits your topic) and a bottom part.
blocks.5.text: Each of those has its own tag. You know them all.
blocks.7.text: One <div> is allowed. Just one, and only if there truly is no tag for what you are building. If you use two — you were being lazy.
blocks.8.text: Not a single <div class="big-text">. Not a single paragraph with bars instead of a menu. Not a single heading that only looks like one.
blocks.10.text: When you are done, do this: close your eyes and read your code out loud, only the tags. "Header. Nav. List of three links. Main. Heading one. Article. Heading two…"
blocks.11.text: If that gives you a sensible picture of the page — you did it. If you hear "group, group, group" — go back and think again.
blocks.12.text: This is not an exercise. It is exactly what someone who cannot see your screen hears.
blocks.14.text: Do not start from the code. Draw the page on paper — rectangles. A strip at the top. Beside or below it — the menu. In the middle — the big part. At the bottom — a narrow strip.
blocks.15.text: Now name each rectangle. If you can name it with a word ("this is the top part"), there is a tag for it. The code is just writing the drawing down.
blocks.17.text: It will be ugly again. You know why by now, and you know it is not your fault.
blocks.18.text: But look at what is left under the ugliness: a page with meaning, that Google understands, a reader can speak, and a human can maintain. The looks are one file away. The meaning cannot be bolted on later.
